title: Trauma and the Inner Ear: Vertigo, Dizziness, and the Vestibular-Nervous System Connection
excerpt: Explore how trauma impacts the inner ear, causing vertigo and dizziness through the vestibular-nervous system link.
body:
Introduction: When Trauma Dizzies the Body

Many people who have experienced trauma find themselves grappling not only with emotional pain but also with confusing physical symptoms like vertigo, dizziness, or a chronic sense of imbalance. These symptoms are often dismissed or misunderstood in traditional therapy settings, leaving sufferers stuck and searching for answers. Recent advances in trauma research and neuroscience reveal a compelling link between trauma and the vestibular system — the inner ear mechanisms responsible for balance and spatial orientation.

This article unpacks the connection between trauma, the vestibular system, and the nervous system. We’ll explore why trauma can literally make you feel dizzy and off-kilter, how the nervous system regulates vestibular function, and what science-backed somatic approaches can help restore balance in body and mind.

The Vestibular System: Your Inner Ear’s Role in Balance and Safety

The vestibular system, located in the inner ear, is the body’s primary sensor for balance, spatial orientation, and movement. It consists of semicircular canals and otolith organs that detect head movements and gravitational forces. This information is continuously transmitted to the brainstem and integrated with visual and proprioceptive inputs to keep you upright and oriented.

Stephen Porges, renowned for his Polyvagal Theory, emphasizes that the vestibular system is deeply connected to the autonomic nervous system, the part of the nervous system that regulates arousal, safety, and social engagement.1 When the vestibular system functions properly, it helps us feel grounded and safe in our bodies and environments.

How Trauma Disrupts Vestibular Function and Nervous System Regulation

Trauma impacts the nervous system’s ability to regulate balance and safety cues. Bessel van der Kolk, in The Body Keeps the Score, describes how traumatic stress physically alters brain circuits including those involved in sensory processing and autonomic regulation.2 The vestibular system, as a key sensory organ linked to these circuits, can become dysregulated by trauma.

Somatic trauma expert Peter Levine explains that trauma often leaves the nervous system stuck in a state of hyperarousal, freeze, or dissociation.3 These survival states interfere with the normal integration of vestibular input. The result? Symptoms like vertigo, dizziness, and a persistent sensation of imbalance that cannot be explained by medical exams alone.

Additionally, Deb Dana’s work on nervous system regulation highlights how trauma can impair the vagus nerve’s ability to modulate vestibular signals and promote calm states.4 Without this modulation, the vestibular system may send exaggerated or conflicting signals to the brain, contributing to disorientation and anxiety.

Why Vertigo and Dizziness Are More Than Inner Ear Problems

Most medical approaches to vertigo focus on structural or mechanical issues in the inner ear, such as benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV) or vestibular neuritis. While these are real conditions, many trauma survivors experience dizziness without identifiable ear pathology.

Pat Ogden and colleagues emphasize that trauma-related vestibular symptoms often stem from nervous system dysregulation rather than ear damage.5 The vestibular system cannot be fully separated from the brain and body’s emotional and safety circuits. When these circuits are flooded or shut down by trauma, the vestibular system’s normal function is compromised.

Gabor Maté also points out that unprocessed trauma disrupts the somatic nervous system’s ability to maintain homeostasis, including balance and spatial orientation.6 This explains why trauma survivors frequently report vertigo-like symptoms that fluctuate with stress levels or emotional triggers.

The Polyvagal Perspective: Vestibular Input and Social Engagement

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory provides a crucial framework for understanding how vestibular function ties into trauma and nervous system regulation.1 The theory describes how the vagus nerve’s ventral branch supports social engagement, safety, and calm, whereas the dorsal branch mediates shutdown responses.

Vestibular input influences vagal tone and can either promote or hinder the nervous system’s ability to shift into a regulated state. Trauma impairs this dynamic, making it difficult to feel grounded and connected. This is why vestibular stimulation techniques (like rocking or swinging) can be powerful tools in somatic healing—they help engage the ventral vagal pathway and restore a sense of safety.

Somatic Approaches to Healing Vestibular Trauma

Understanding the trauma-vestibular connection opens the door to targeted somatic healing strategies. Here are several approaches supported by research and clinical practice:

  • Vestibular Stimulation Exercises: Gentle rocking, swinging, or balance activities can help retrain the vestibular system and improve nervous system regulation. These practices engage the vagus nerve and promote safety.4
  • Somatic Experiencing (SE): Developed by Peter Levine, SE focuses on completing the body’s natural trauma response cycles, including regulating sensory input from the vestibular system.3
  • Sensorimotor Psychotherapy: Pat Ogden’s approach integrates body awareness and movement to process trauma and restore nervous system balance.5
  • Mindfulness and Grounding Techniques: These practices help bring attention to the body and vestibular sensations, reducing dissociation and hypervigilance.7
  • Polyvagal-Informed Therapy: Using principles from Deb Dana’s work, therapists guide clients in exercises that enhance vagal tone and vestibular-autonomic integration.4

Practical Tips for Self-Directed Healing

If you’re experiencing trauma-related vertigo or dizziness, here are actionable steps you can try at home to support your vestibular system and nervous system:

  • Engage in slow, rhythmic rocking motions—use a rocking chair or gently sway your body back and forth.
  • Practice balance exercises like standing on one foot, walking heel-to-toe, or using a balance board if available.
  • Incorporate breathwork focusing on slow, deep exhalations to activate the parasympathetic nervous system.
  • Explore gentle yoga poses that emphasize balance and orientation, such as tree pose or downward dog.
  • Use grounding techniques like feeling your feet on the floor or holding a textured object to anchor yourself.

Remember, these practices should be done gently and mindfully. If vertigo or dizziness intensifies, pause and consult a healthcare professional knowledgeable in trauma-informed care.

“Trauma doesn’t just live in the mind—it gets lodged in the body’s systems, including the vestibular apparatus, affecting how we move, feel, and stay safe.” – The Oracle Lover

When to Seek Professional Help

While self-directed somatic work can be empowering, persistent or severe vestibular symptoms warrant evaluation by professionals. Look for clinicians trained in trauma-informed vestibular rehabilitation, somatic therapies, or integrative approaches.

Francine Shapiro’s EMDR therapy, which targets sensory processing, can also be beneficial for trauma-related vestibular symptoms by reprocessing traumatic memories and their somatic imprint.8 Combining somatic therapies with traditional medical care provides the best chance for restoring balance and resilience.

Conclusion: Integrating Trauma Healing and Vestibular Health

Trauma’s impact on the inner ear and vestibular system is a critical piece of the healing puzzle for many stuck in cycles of vertigo, dizziness, and imbalance. By recognizing the vestibular-nervous system connection, we can move beyond purely mechanical explanations and address the somatic roots of these symptoms.

Grounded in the work of van der Kolk, Levine, Porges, Dana, Ogden, Maté, Siegel, Schwartz, and Shapiro, somatic healing approaches offer pathways to restore safety, regulation, and embodiment. Trauma-informed vestibular care can help you regain your balance literally and figuratively—reclaiming your body’s ability to feel steady, safe, and present.

References

  1. Porges, S. W. (2011). The polyvagal theory: Neurophysiological foundations of emotions, attachment, communication, and self-regulation. W.W. Norton.
  2. van der Kolk, B. (2015). The Body Keeps the Score: Brain, Mind, and Body in the Healing of Trauma. Penguin Books.
  3. Levine, P. A. (2010). In an Unspoken Voice: How the Body Releases Trauma and Restores Goodness. North Atlantic Books.
  4. Dana, D. (2018). The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy: Engaging the Rhythm of Regulation. Norton & Company.
  5. Ogden, P., Minton, K., & Pain, C. (2006). Trauma and the Body: A Sensorimotor Approach to Psychotherapy. W.W. Norton.
  6. Maté, G. (2018). When the Body Says No: The Cost of Hidden Stress. Wiley.
  7. Siegel, D. J. (2012). The Developing Mind: How Relationships and the Brain Interact to Shape Who We Are. Guilford Press.
  8. Shapiro, F. (2017). Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) Therapy: Basic Principles, Protocols, and Procedures (3rd ed.). Guilford Press.

faqs.q: Can trauma cause vertigo even if my inner ear is healthy?
faqs.a: Yes. Trauma can dysregulate the nervous system and vestibular processing, causing vertigo or dizziness even when medical tests show a healthy inner ear.

faqs.q: How does the vagus nerve influence balance and dizziness?
faqs.a: The vagus nerve modulates autonomic nervous system states, influencing vestibular signals and helping regulate feelings of safety and balance.

faqs.q: What somatic exercises can help with trauma-related dizziness?
faqs.a: Gentle rocking, balance exercises, breathwork, and grounding techniques can support vestibular regulation and nervous system calming.

faqs.q: When should I see a professional for my vertigo symptoms?
faqs.a: If dizziness is persistent, severe, or worsening, consult a healthcare provider trained in trauma-informed care and vestibular rehabilitation.
